title: ASP.NET Intro
content:
ASP.NET Introduction

What is ASP.NET?

ASP.NET is a free, open-source web framework created by Microsoft for building modern web applications and services using .NET and C#. It is the successor to classic ASP (Active Server Pages).

  • Build dynamic web pages and APIs
  • Cross-platform (Windows, macOS, Linux)
  • High performance and scalable
  • Built-in security features

ASP.NET vs Classic ASP

Classic ASP used VBScript and had limited features. ASP.NET uses C# with a powerful framework, offering MVC architecture, Razor syntax, dependency injection, and much more.

title: ASP.NET Setup
content:
ASP.NET Environment Setup

Prerequisites

  • .NET SDK: Download from dotnet.microsoft.com
  • Visual Studio or VS Code
  • C# Extension for VS Code (optional)

Create Your First Project

Use the .NET CLI to create a new ASP.NET web app in seconds.

CLI Commands
# Create a new web app
dotnet new webapp -o MyWebApp

# Navigate into the project
cd MyWebApp

# Run the application
dotnet run

title: Project Structure
content:
ASP.NET Project Structure

Key Files & Folders

  • Program.cs: The entry point of your application. Configures services and the HTTP request pipeline.
  • wwwroot/: Contains static files (CSS, JS, images).
  • Pages/ or Views/: Contains Razor pages or MVC views.
  • appsettings.json: Application configuration (connection strings, logging, etc.).
  • *.csproj: The project file listing dependencies and build settings.

Program.cs Example

Minimal API Entry Point
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);
var app = builder.Build();

app.MapGet("/", () => "Hello, ASP.NET!");

app.Run();

title: Razor Pages
content:
ASP.NET Razor Pages

What are Razor Pages?

Razor Pages is a page-based programming model in ASP.NET that makes building web UI easier. Each page has an .cshtml file (HTML + C#) and a .cshtml.cs code-behind file.

Razor Syntax

Use the @ symbol to switch from HTML to C# code inside a Razor page.

Razor Page Example (.cshtml)
@page
@model IndexModel

<h1>Welcome, @Model.UserName!</h1>

@if (Model.IsLoggedIn)
{
    <p>You are logged in.</p>
}
else
{
    <p>Please log in.</p>
}

@for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)
{
    <p>Item @i</p>
}

title: MVC Pattern
content:
ASP.NET MVC Pattern

Model-View-Controller

MVC separates an application into three components:

  • Model: Represents the data and business logic.
  • View: Displays the UI (Razor templates).
  • Controller: Handles user requests and returns responses.

Controller Example

HomeController.cs
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc;

public class HomeController : Controller
{
    public IActionResult Index()
    {
        ViewBag.Message = "Hello from ASP.NET MVC!";
        return View();
    }

    public IActionResult About()
    {
        return View();
    }
}

title: Routing
content:
ASP.NET Routing

How Routing Works

Routing maps incoming HTTP requests to specific controller actions or endpoints. ASP.NET supports both conventional routing and attribute routing.

Attribute Routing

Routing Examples
// Attribute Routing
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class ProductsController : Controller
{
    [HttpGet]              // GET /api/products
    public IActionResult GetAll() => Ok(products);

    [HttpGet("{id}")]      // GET /api/products/5
    public IActionResult GetById(int id) => Ok(product);

    [HttpPost]             // POST /api/products
    public IActionResult Create([FromBody] Product p) => Ok(p);
}

// Conventional Routing (in Program.cs)
app.MapControllerRoute(
    name: "default",
    pattern: "{controller=Home}/{action=Index}/{id?}"
);

title: Models & Data
content:
ASP.NET Models & Data Binding

Creating a Model

A Model is a C# class that represents the data structure. Data annotations can be used for validation.

Model Example
using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;

public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    [Required]
    [StringLength(100)]
    public string Name { get; set; }

    [Range(0.01, 10000)]
    public decimal Price { get; set; }

    public string Description { get; set; }
}

Model Binding

ASP.NET automatically maps form data, query strings, and JSON bodies to your model parameters — this is called Model Binding.

title: Views & Layouts
content:
ASP.NET Views & Layouts

Razor Views

Views are .cshtml files that contain the HTML and Razor syntax to render the UI.

Shared Layout

The _Layout.cshtml file defines a common template (header, footer, nav) shared across all pages.

_Layout.cshtml
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    <title>@ViewData["Title"] - My App</title>
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="~/css/site.css" />
</head>
<body>
    <nav>...navigation...</nav>

    <main>
        @RenderBody()
    </main>

    <footer>© 2026 My App</footer>
</body>
</html>

title: Forms & Validation
content:
ASP.NET Forms & Validation

HTML Forms with Tag Helpers

ASP.NET Tag Helpers make it easy to create forms that bind directly to your models.

Form Example
<form asp-action="Create" method="post">
    <label asp-for="Name"></label>
    <input asp-for="Name" />
    <span asp-validation-for="Name"></span>

    <label asp-for="Price"></label>
    <input asp-for="Price" />
    <span asp-validation-for="Price"></span>

    <button type="submit">Create</button>
</form>

Server-Side Validation

Check ModelState.IsValid in your controller to validate data annotations on the model before saving.

Validation in Controller
[HttpPost]
public IActionResult Create(Product product)
{
    if (!ModelState.IsValid)
    {
        return View(product); // Return form with errors
    }
    // Save product to database
    return RedirectToAction("Index");
}

title: Dependency Injection
content:
ASP.NET Dependency Injection

What is DI?

Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern built into ASP.NET Core. Instead of creating dependencies manually, the framework injects them for you, making code testable and loosely coupled.

Registering & Using Services

DI Example
// 1. Define an interface and implementation
public interface IGreetingService
{
    string Greet(string name);
}

public class GreetingService : IGreetingService
{
    public string Greet(string name) => $"Hello, {name}!";
}

// 2. Register in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddScoped<IGreetingService, GreetingService>();

// 3. Inject into a Controller
public class HomeController : Controller
{
    private readonly IGreetingService _greeter;

    public HomeController(IGreetingService greeter)
    {
        _greeter = greeter;
    }

    public IActionResult Index()
    {
        ViewBag.Message = _greeter.Greet("World");
        return View();
    }
}

title: Entity Framework
content:
Entity Framework Core

What is EF Core?

Entity Framework Core is an ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) that lets you interact with databases using C# objects instead of writing raw SQL queries.

DbContext & CRUD

EF Core Example
// DbContext
public class AppDbContext : DbContext
{
    public DbSet<Product> Products { get; set; }

    public AppDbContext(DbContextOptions<AppDbContext> options)
        : base(options) { }
}

// Register in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(options =>
    options.UseSqlServer(builder.Configuration
        .GetConnectionString("Default")));

// CRUD in Controller
public class ProductsController : Controller
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;
    public ProductsController(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;

    public IActionResult Index() => View(_db.Products.ToList());

    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Create(Product p)
    {
        _db.Products.Add(p);
        _db.SaveChanges();
        return RedirectToAction("Index");
    }
}

title: Middleware
content:
ASP.NET Middleware

What is Middleware?

Middleware is software assembled into the app pipeline to handle requests and responses. Each component can process the request, pass it to the next component, or short-circuit the pipeline.

  • Authentication & Authorization
  • Logging & Error Handling
  • Static File Serving
  • CORS, Routing, Sessions

Custom Middleware

Middleware Pipeline
// Built-in middleware in Program.cs
app.UseHttpsRedirection();
app.UseStaticFiles();
app.UseRouting();
app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();

// Custom inline middleware
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    Console.WriteLine($"Request: {context.Request.Path}");
    await next();  // Call the next middleware
    Console.WriteLine($"Response: {context.Response.StatusCode}");
});

app.MapControllers();
app.Run();

title: Web API
content:
ASP.NET Web API

Building RESTful APIs

ASP.NET makes it easy to build RESTful APIs that return JSON data. API controllers inherit from ControllerBase and use attributes like [ApiController].

API Controller Example

REST API
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class TodoController : ControllerBase
{
    private static List<string> todos = new() { "Learn ASP.NET", "Build API" };

    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll() => Ok(todos);

    [HttpGet("{id}")]
    public IActionResult GetById(int id)
    {
        if (id >= todos.Count) return NotFound();
        return Ok(todos[id]);
    }

    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Add([FromBody] string todo)
    {
        todos.Add(todo);
        return CreatedAtAction(nameof(GetById),
            new { id = todos.Count - 1 }, todo);
    }

    [HttpDelete("{id}")]
    public IActionResult Delete(int id)
    {
        todos.RemoveAt(id);
        return NoContent();
    }
}

title: Authentication
content:
ASP.NET Authentication

Identity System

ASP.NET Identity is a membership system that handles user registration, login, roles, and claims. It supports cookie-based auth, JWT tokens, and external providers (Google, Facebook).

Securing Endpoints

Auth Examples
// Add Identity in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddDefaultIdentity<IdentityUser>()
    .AddEntityFrameworkStores<AppDbContext>();

builder.Services.AddAuthentication()
    .AddCookie();

// Protect a controller or action
[Authorize]
public class DashboardController : Controller
{
    public IActionResult Index() => View();
}

// Allow anonymous access
[AllowAnonymous]
public IActionResult PublicPage() => View();

// Role-based authorization
[Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]
public IActionResult AdminPanel() => View();

title: Error Handling
content:
ASP.NET Error Handling

Exception Handling Middleware

ASP.NET provides built-in middleware for handling exceptions globally, showing developer-friendly error pages in development and user-friendly pages in production.

Global Error Handling

Error Handling Setup
// In Program.cs
if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
{
    app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage(); // Detailed errors
}
else
{
    app.UseExceptionHandler("/Home/Error"); // Custom error page
    app.UseHsts();
}

// Custom Exception Filter
public class GlobalExceptionFilter : IExceptionFilter
{
    public void OnException(ExceptionContext context)
    {
        var result = new { error = context.Exception.Message };
        context.Result = new JsonResult(result)
        {
            StatusCode = 500
        };
        context.ExceptionHandled = true;
    }
}

title: Configuration
content:
ASP.NET Configuration

appsettings.json

ASP.NET uses appsettings.json for configuration. You can store connection strings, API keys, and custom settings. Environment-specific files like appsettings.Development.json override values per environment.

Reading Configuration

Configuration Example
// appsettings.json
{
  "ConnectionStrings": {
    "Default": "Server=.;Database=MyDb;Trusted_Connection=true;"
  },
  "AppSettings": {
    "SiteName": "My ASP.NET App",
    "MaxItems": 100
  }
}

// Read in Program.cs or Controller
var siteName = builder.Configuration["AppSettings:SiteName"];
var connStr = builder.Configuration
    .GetConnectionString("Default");

// Strongly typed with Options pattern
public class AppSettings
{
    public string SiteName { get; set; }
    public int MaxItems { get; set; }
}

builder.Services.Configure<AppSettings>(
    builder.Configuration.GetSection("AppSettings"));

title: Logging
content:
ASP.NET Logging

Built-in Logging

ASP.NET Core has a built-in logging framework that supports multiple providers (Console, Debug, EventLog) and log levels (Trace, Debug, Information, Warning, Error, Critical).

Using ILogger

Logging Example
public class HomeController : Controller
{
    private readonly ILogger<HomeController> _logger;

    public HomeController(ILogger<HomeController> logger)
    {
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public IActionResult Index()
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Home page visited.");
        _logger.LogWarning("This is a warning.");
        _logger.LogError("Something went wrong!");
        return View();
    }
}

title: Minimal APIs
content:
ASP.NET Minimal APIs

What are Minimal APIs?

Minimal APIs were introduced in .NET 6 to simplify building lightweight HTTP APIs without the overhead of controllers. Everything is defined in Program.cs.

Minimal API Example

Full Minimal API
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);
var app = builder.Build();

var todos = new List<string> { "Learn C#", "Build API" };

app.MapGet("/todos", () => Results.Ok(todos));

app.MapGet("/todos/{id}", (int id) =>
    id < todos.Count
        ? Results.Ok(todos[id])
        : Results.NotFound());

app.MapPost("/todos", (string todo) =>
{
    todos.Add(todo);
    return Results.Created($"/todos/{todos.Count - 1}", todo);
});

app.MapDelete("/todos/{id}", (int id) =>
{
    todos.RemoveAt(id);
    return Results.NoContent();
});

app.Run();

title: SignalR
content:
ASP.NET SignalR

Real-Time Communication

SignalR is a library for adding real-time web functionality. It uses WebSockets under the hood and is perfect for chat apps, live dashboards, and notifications.

SignalR Hub Example

Chat Hub
// ChatHub.cs
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.SignalR;

public class ChatHub : Hub
{
    public async Task SendMessage(string user, string message)
    {
        await Clients.All.SendAsync(
            "ReceiveMessage", user, message);
    }
}

// Register in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddSignalR();
app.MapHub<ChatHub>("/chatHub");

// Client-side JavaScript
// const connection = new signalR.HubConnectionBuilder()
//     .withUrl("/chatHub").build();
// connection.on("ReceiveMessage", (user, msg) => { ... });
// connection.start();

title: Deployment
content:
ASP.NET Deployment

Publishing Your App

ASP.NET apps can be deployed to IIS, Azure App Service, Docker containers, or any Linux server with the .NET runtime installed.

  • Azure App Service: One-click deploy from Visual Studio or GitHub Actions.
  • Docker: Containerize for consistent cross-platform deployment.
  • IIS: Traditional Windows hosting with full .NET support.
  • Linux: Run with Kestrel behind Nginx or Apache as a reverse proxy.

Publish Commands

Deployment Commands
# Publish for production
dotnet publish -c Release -o ./publish

# Docker Example
# Dockerfile
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0
COPY ./publish /app
WORKDIR /app
ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "MyWebApp.dll"]

# Build & run Docker container
docker build -t mywebapp .
docker run -p 8080:80 mywebapp

title: Course Summary
content:
ASP.NET Complete Course Summary 🚀

You Did It! 🏆

You have completed the ASP.NET course! From setting up your first project to building APIs, handling authentication, and deploying to production — you now have a solid foundation in Microsoft's powerful web framework.

  • Fundamentals: Project Structure, Razor Pages, MVC Pattern, Routing.
  • Data & Logic: Models, Entity Framework Core, Forms & Validation.
  • Architecture: Dependency Injection, Middleware, Configuration, Logging.
  • Advanced: Web APIs, Minimal APIs, SignalR, Authentication.
  • DevOps: Deployment to Azure, Docker, IIS, and Linux.

You are now ready to build production-grade web applications with ASP.NET. Keep coding and ship great software!
